we are going to be talking about staffing based on class. So how are we gonna staff? Who are we gonna hire? Who do we need? And all of that stuff based on the specific class that you are in. So let's dive right into it. Focus on higher value tasks, okay? We don't wanna over staff, we don't wanna over pay. So we're staying as lean as we can as we grow before we have to make the next acquisition to continue growing. If we don't, we probably will stop growing. So that is what this model is based on is staying lean and then acquiring right before the bottleneck but to keep on growing. Now, exact details on what each person does, how they're acquired, paid, et cetera. That is going to be covered in the team training course and the owner course in the portal, formerly known as Levels of Legacy and Leadership. But we are going to be going over just an overview of each class. So let's jump right into the overview. We'll start with Class A. Class A is zero to 100 members and at this point their staff should be pretty small. Maybe they just opened up or they have just, they've been in business for a little bit and not focused on a massive scaling. Maybe they just got into gym launch. Anything like that, they should have an owner. Trainers should actually say one to two trainers. So one trainer is fine, one to two trainers is fine. And then a part-time admin to do things like answer the phone and book appointments and nurture leads and things like that. Just a part-time admin, 20 hours a week, nothing crazy. That's basically all that's required at that level. Now, at this level, the owner will likely be coaching still but should be reserved for mornings only. So try to coach only in the mornings, nothing in the evening because we need that time for sales. So this is why trainers are needed at this level instead of just a one man show because they'll need to coach the morning and evening classes. So just morning, if the owner has the evenings just completely covered, I'm sorry, vice versa, just the trainers just need to do the evenings if the owner has the mornings completely covered but one to two will really help out and it gives diversity to the group instead of just people showing up and only getting to work with the same person. So based on the volume, there shouldn't be a need for a ton of classes per week. 20 of them should be good. I mean, that's less than two, four, that's less than four a day. If you're running six days a week, most people are closed on Sundays or something like that, two in the morning, two in the evening, that should be sufficient for the less than 100 members. So one to two trainers should be able to handle that. Now, this allows the owner to sell in the evenings while also managing the other employees and scaling the business. So at this point in time, the owner should be really owning lead gen, sales, challenge fulfillment, member retention and operations, pretty much doing it all, wearing all the hats. That's what it's like when you start off a business. So the trainers can provide assistance with each of the pieces, but they don't fully own the outcome. It's still, the buck still is settled with the stops with the owner, right? You're not gonna say, you know, trainer, you're responsible for our acquisition. It's too early on, they can't handle that. So at this point, the owner still owns all the outcomes. The admin part-time can help with the lowest value tasks that take up the most time and attention. That's what they're there for, just the noise that is the lowest paid, highest volume stuff. Examples would be billing in collections, tracking numbers like challenge or visits or weigh-ins, making copies of paperwork, light cleaning, checking people in the class, all that kind of stuff is perfect for an admin to get started with. Now, if hiring for this class, attempt to find a part-time admin that, A, you see possibly growing into the role, like an ops manager down the road, or B, they want to grow and go from part-time to full-time, because that eventually will happen. So when you're interviewing or hiring this person, make sure that you see them growing and they see themselves growing. Otherwise, you might have to rehire when you get to that point. So this can, I already said that, this can save you from having to rehire and retain, or I'm sorry, retrain them later on. Now, class B, 100 to 175 members, we have our owner, our trainers bump up to two to three, admin is still part-time, and now we have acquired a challenge manager or coach, whatever you want to call them, same role. Now, at this level, the owner should be coaching only as a backup for trainers needing off for an emergency or something like that, but they shouldn't be doing those morning classes anymore. That's why another trainer is required to take the morning classes. This addition allows the owner to sell in the mornings and the evenings. Okay, so based on the volume, there should be about 25 to 30 classes per week, so two to three trainers should be good at that point. We want to sell in the mornings as well, because there's a lot of people that can only work out in the morning, and so if you don't have any availability listed in the mornings to sell, they may never even come into your gym, because they'll just look at your scheduler and none of the times work. So even a couple days would be a huge help. Now, the admin's job does not change, they're still doing the same thing. However, a dedicated challenge manager or coach, 20 hours a week, will be needed to fulfill the increased amount of challengers from the owner selling in the morning and the evening. So the previous step, we had the owner shifted to selling more, so now because of that, there's more challengers. And so the owner needs somebody to fulfill these challengers, and if you don't have somebody dedicated to doing it, they will likely not get as good of an experience, which means you're not gonna be retaining as many of them. So there's no point in signing up all these challengers if you're not going to retain them. The owner has more attention to sell and scale, which equals more challengers, and the, or it equals more closes, and more closes are more challengers, and the challenge manager is keeping more of those challengers, because that's their job. So now because you have the owner selling more and a challenge manager primarily focused on retaining all the challengers, you are starting to get a lot more members. So we need to bring on a retention manager to keep those members. So there needs to be somebody that is dedicated to this, to the fulfillment, and the retention of them, hence the retention manager. Their job is to take retention systems off of the owner's back and own retention. Okay, so they're gonna run all the systems that are, they should, the systems should already be in place, right? The four, the five horsemen of retention, those should already be in place, but the owner is no longer managing all those systems and running them all. That's what you're hiring the retention manager for. So at this level, the owner should be owning lead gen, sales, and still operations, mostly, with some admin assistance. The reason they're not taking it over fully yet is because they do have that admin helping them, and you don't want to start losing members. Next, class D, dun dun dun, 250 to 325 members, we are getting after it. So at the gym, we have an owner, trainers, this time we're at five to six, we're starting, we have more classes. The ops manager now is full-time, so that admin changed from admin part-time to ops manager full-time. And then the challenge manager and retention manager stay exactly the same. So at this level, the owner should not be coaching, running the challenge groups or the retention systems. Those are all delegated. Adding another trainer or two will help with the increased classes needed to service the increased amount of members. So as you scale, as you keep adding more and more members, you'll need to add more classes because they can't just all fit into the original 20 classes you had. It's not gonna, it'll be packed. So that's why we keep adding trainers, is because when you add classes, typically you need to add trainers to service those, and it's great for diversity and keeping the classes changing. So based on that, you should be at about 40 to 50 classes per week, so five to six trainers should be good fit for that. Now, the challenge manager's job does not change at all. The retention manager's job does not change at all. The admin gets promoted to ops manager, right? So if they or you don't want to promote them, if they don't wanna get promoted, if you don't think they're a good fit for full-time, that's when you need to hire somebody at 40 hours. And you don't have two admins at 20 each. It doesn't, you won't get the ownership that you'll need with this role, because this ops manager is very, very important. So this is a big hire for you. So if you don't feel comfortable with this person, then you need to let them go, or let them go, and hire somebody at 40 hours because the ops manager really handles the day-to-day stuff, and that will really come in handy when you don't wanna do it anymore. So at this level, the owner would be really focusing on sales and then setting up and improving all the retention systems. So the retention systems get more advanced when trying to deliver the same service you were able to for 100 members and now trying to do it at 300 members. So they'll really need to keep focusing on retention. Back at Class A when you have 100 members, like it's not that hard. You can text all of them back, you know? I mean, it's not that hard to retain them because there's not too many of them, but at 300 members, it just becomes more difficult. So the owner needs to put his attention there, right? That is where the most value is. In addition, the owner has a larger staff to manage with more direct reports. So now, I mean, at this point, everyone's still reporting to the owner. So if somebody gets sick, somebody needs to go on vacation, somebody has an issue with a client, another employee, whatever, it all is going to the owner. So, and that stuff happens. It would be amazing to have a workplace where none of that stuff happens, but it does. So the owner still has all these direct reports and they need to put their attention there. So this is why an admin must be promoted from part-time to full-time to take over the remaining administrative duties the owner was doing. They are done with all of the admin stuff, whether it's bookkeeping or accounting or dealing with entering contracts or billing or whatever they were still doing admin-wise, they're done doing that. So the full list of duties in Jim Launch Training Portal. So everything that an ops manager does, which is a lot, is located in the team training portal. So you can look through it and see that what they should be doing. Now, at this level, the owner should still be owning lead gen and sales, right? But challenge fulfillment, member retention, operations, that's all delegated, that's all delegated. Class E, we are getting up there. 325 to 400 members. Now, at this point, we have an owner, trainers, so maybe six to seven trainers, an ops manager full-time, a challenge manager and coach, a retention manager, and now we are bringing on a sales manager, one or two of them. Let's dig into that piece. So at this level, the owner should not be coaching, running the challenge groups, retention systems or operations. We are nearing, we're at seven figures. So adding another trainer or two will help with the increased classes needed to service the increased amount of members. So that's why having two people is better than one because you're not putting all your faith into one person and competition amongst sales equals better results. That's why there's always a sales team and not just one person, because you don't wanna get into a situation where the sales person is like, all these leads suck. And it's like, they don't suck and I used to close all of them. And they're like, well, they suck now. If you have two people selling and one of them is closing at 70% on a regular basis or 60% on a regular basis, the other person cannot say the leads suck. They just are not closing them, but the person next to them is. That's why it's so much more advantageous to hire two of them. If you have somebody that is an absolute beast after 30, 60 days, you could promote them and get rid of the other one. So, because at that point, you've already set the expectations, right? You know what they can close that. They know what they can close that. Now we just wanna double their volume and the other person can go. But I wouldn't recommend starting off with one because they don't have that strong base. So, if you do have to do one, for whatever reason, find the best candidate possible. They should start off with one-on-one or small group sales before moving into a large group, really to get used to everything. And large groups are harder to close and not many other industries where these people will have experience have done a lot of large group sales before. So, at this level, the owner should be simply owning lead gen. They are still making it rain. They're still placing ads and all that stuff, but sales, challenge fulfillment, member retention and ops have been delegated. Class F, the big daddy, 400 plus members. At this point, you have an owner, trainers, seven to eight probably to fulfill everything. An ops manager, full-time, a challenge manager, retention manager, sales managers, and now we are bringing on our final acquisition, the final pieces of the puzzle, the front desk and the lead trainer. Why do we need these people? I will tell you. At this level, the owner should not be coaching, running the challenge groups, retention systems, operations or sales. They should have a seven-figure gym and are now rain making, okay? So, adding another trainer or two will help with the increased classes needed to service the increased amount of members. So, based on the volume, you should be at about 60 to 70 classes per week. 60 is probably great at 400. And so, seven to eight trainers should be good. The challenge manager's job does not change. The retention manager's job does not change. The ops manager's job does not change. The sales manager's job does not change. None of these things changes. You're just adding another person to take more away from the owner. That's all you're doing. So, dedicated front desk is hired and the lead trainer is promoted. Why are we doing that? At this level, the owner will now need to have less direct reports and more leaders on the team. Okay, so the head trainer now serves as the leader of the training team and relieves that duty from the owner. Because before that, all the trainers were reporting to the owner, right? And at least in this system they were. And so, that is like seven to eight direct reports. And that's not even including the other managers, right? So, that's too many. A good leader can only have four to five direct reports. They can have direct reports themselves, but that you can't overload somebody with 16 different people's problems and things they need. It's too much. So, the head trainer is absorbing the, they're acting as a new piece in the chain of command between the training staff and the owner. Okay, now, the dedicated front desk, they absorb noise. They absorb noise and attention from your 400 plus members, which think about how many people are hanging out at the front desk with 100 members. When you get to 400, there is always people up there talking, just chatty, cat, always talking. And the managers, they have so much to do at this level. They have, I mean, they have systems to run, they have emails to get back to, they're doing all sales, they're doing everything. So, you can't have them behind the front desk, which they were until now. Until now, you should have had one of your managers. Like, I've always had my challenge manager behind the front desk, working the front desk while they're doing all their challenge manager stuff, because that is great for building rapport and like getting to know everybody. Your retention manager is another great person to have behind the front desk, because they are working with members all the time too and sending them cards and all that stuff. So, either one of those are great to have behind the front desk. But at some point, the volume's so high that they just can't do their job, you know? And so, that's why we need to bring somebody on to absorb that attention, $10 an hour, something like that, because the other managers are making more money and you need them to be 100% focused on what they're supposed to be doing and not talking to Susie about her dog, you know? And that's the point of having somebody dedicated. So, at this point, you want to hire those two or promote those two, you promote the trainer, you hire the sales manager, the dedicated front desk, you hire them to absorb attention. Now, the owner now has four direct reports. Okay, we went from having all of them to only four. So, the gym is now a team of teams. It is one team of smaller teams and that is how you divide and conquer. And this is how you are able to delegate, command, you're able to delegate leadership and the owner can just focus on making these for the best leader they can. So, sales manager, challenge manager, ops manager, retention manager. Those are the big four that are running your gym. The lead trainer is great, especially if you have a great one because they are going to be leading the coaching staff. The accountability coach, I have one that works alongside the retention manager. It's just one of my trainers. But the retention manager is very, very good at administrative stuff and cards going out and everything. They don't know much about like actual fitness and nutrition. So, I pair an accountability coach with them for like 10 hours a week, just to honestly answer questions about fitness, nutrition, anything that our members have questions about that is outside of these four people, they're all admin, they're not trainers. You don't cross, I mean, we already went over that. So, you don't cross trainers with admin. I mean, the personality types, we already trained on that, but don't do it. So, in some cases, you need to have somebody that knows exercise, science and nutrition because of the questions coming in. So, that brings in the accountability coach for retention and the challenge coach, whatever for the challenge manager, right? Again, it's just another one of my coaches that said, I'll do it for an extra $10 or 10 hours a week. They answer all the nutrition, recovery, all that stuff that the challenge manager can't answer or doesn't feel comfortable answering and the challenge manager's doing all the admin work. So, that's how these work. And this is what you should look like when you are at 400 plus members and everything will run smooth. That was cool, let's move on. Okay, the owner at this point can focus on working on the business and not in the business. Okay, they're doing no day-to-day tasks and everything has been delegated. Okay, Lee Jen can be taught to another staff member, which I've done, and it's just exactly how you learned it. That's how you teach it, same thing. You just teach someone else to do it. Or the owner can continue doing it themselves if they'd like, which every now and then I do create new ads because it's awesome and I wanna see how good I am at it. So, it's completely up to the owner what they wanna do at this point. Lee Jen is not, it shouldn't take a ton of time. So, at this level, the owner should be owning none of this stuff and they should just be rain making. They should be doing what they need to do at a high level to continually drive that company forward. Really, it becomes working on making your leaders better leaders and making them own. Your sales managers should own acquisition. If you have a goal for acquisition for the month and you don't hit it, it's not the owner that feels bad. It should be that person. And they're like, man, dude, that's my livelihood. Sales, acquisition, same thing with retention. You have a month where you're at 8% retention. Like that person should care enough to where it like ruins their day. And instead of the owner being like, man, what are we gonna, you want that person to feel it. And that's when you create leaders and that's what the owner should be focusing on at that point.
